name: Fairview
short_description: Free BLM rockhounding in central Utah's scenic Sanpete County. Collect agate, jasper, and quartz specimens with a 25 lb daily limit and no permit required.
description:
Fairview offers outstanding rockhounding on public BLM land in the heart of Utah's Sanpete County. This area sits within the Basin and Range province where Miocene-era volcanic activity and silica-rich hydrothermal fluids deposited extensive agate and jasper formations. The landscape features rolling foothills and wash systems that have naturally exposed colorful specimens over thousands of years of erosion.

Collectors typically find banded and dendritic agates, red and golden jaspers, and clear to smoky quartz in the washes and along hillside outcrops. The terrain is relatively gentle with good foot access to producing areas, making it suitable for rockhounds of varying experience levels. The open desert environment provides excellent visibility of specimens, especially after seasonal flooding exposes fresh material.

Being on BLM land means personal use collection is welcome with minimal restrictions - no permits, fees, or prior approval required. The area rarely becomes crowded, giving you space to explore at your own pace. Spring runoff often refreshes the washes with newly exposed specimens, though the site can be productive year-round for patient collectors.
history: This region's mineral wealth stems from extensive Tertiary-age volcanic and hydrothermal activity that blanketed central Utah with silica-rich deposits. The Sanpete Valley and surrounding foothills are part of the Basin and Range volcanic province, where agate and jasper formed in veins and nodules within ancient rhyolitic and basaltic flows and associated alteration zones.
tips: Search the bottoms and banks of ephemeral washes where water has concentrated and sorted colorful specimens - agates and jaspers often collect in small gravel deposits. Use a 10x loupe to examine potential finds in place before collecting, as high-quality banded or dendritic pieces are more valuable than simple solid chunks. Look for pieces with strong color saturation and visible banding or patterns rather than plain gray or brown material. A rock hammer and cold chisel help extract embedded specimens from hardpan and outcrop, but most surface finds can be picked up by hand.
terrain_notes: The Fairview area consists of rolling foothills at approximately 5,400-5,800 feet elevation with scattered sagebrush and ponderosa pine. Terrain is moderately sloped with sandy and rocky soil, making footing generally stable but requiring sturdy boots. Walking distances from parking to productive areas typically range from 0.5 to 2 miles. Washes are shallow and non-technical, though winter or spring runoff can temporarily obscure access.
best_season: April - October. Spring months (April-May) are ideal as recent snowmelt refreshes washes with exposed material and temperatures are mild. Summer heat (June-August) is moderate at elevation but afternoon thunderstorms can be sudden. Fall (September-October) offers excellent weather and reduced crowds. Winter access is possible but mountain snow may close higher access roads.
written_directions: From Manti (the nearest town with services, approximately 15 miles south), take US 89 north toward Fairview. Continue through Fairview on Main Street. Turn east on a signed BLM access road (approximately 2-3 miles north of Fairview town center). Follow the dirt road for 3-5 miles to parking pullouts near productive wash areas. High-clearance vehicles are helpful after heavy rain, but standard passenger vehicles can usually access main parking areas.
road_conditions: Dirt access roads in good condition during dry months. May develop washboard and minor ruts after rain. Passenger vehicles acceptable to main areas; high-clearance preferred for secondary access roads.
parking_notes: Multiple informal pullouts along the access road and near wash entrances. No developed parking lot, but ample space for 5-10 vehicles at common collection areas. No facilities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 pounds per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection permitted. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment use. No removal of specimens larger than approximately 5 pounds without prior BLM authorization. Respect marked claim boundaries and avoid any private inholdings.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 pounds per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Respect all claim boundaries
Leave no trash
nearest_services: Manti, approximately 15 miles south, offers gas, food, water, and supplies. Sanpete Valley Hospital is in Manti.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently rolling terrain with stable footing in most areas. Uneven rocky wash bottoms may be challenging for those with limited mobility. No wheelchair access to collection areas. Sturdy hiking boots recommended. Distances are short (under 2 miles), making the site accessible to most hikers with average fitness.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding near Fairview, Utah?
faq.answer: The primary finds are banded and dendritic agates, red and golden jaspers, and clear to smoky quartz. These form in the volcanic deposits throughout Sanpete County's foothills. Agate is the most sought-after, particularly pieces with strong color and clear banding patterns.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Fairview?
faq.answer: No permit is required. Fairview sits on BLM public land where personal use collection is allowed without advance permission or fees. You must follow the 25 pound per day limit and avoid commercial use.

faq.question: What is the best season for rockhounding in Fairview, Utah?
faq.answer: April through October is ideal, with spring (April-May) being optimal. Recent snowmelt refreshes washes and exposes fresh specimens, while temperatures remain mild. Fall offers excellent weather and fewer crowds. Summer is workable but afternoon thunderstorms can develop quickly at this elevation.

faq.question: How do I get to Fairview rockhounding area from Manti?
faq.answer: From Manti, take US 89 north through Fairview. Turn east on a signed BLM access road 2-3 miles north of town and follow the dirt road 3-5 miles to parking areas near productive washes. Passenger vehicles can access main parking, though high-clearance is helpful after rain.

faq.question: Is Fairview a good rockhounding spot for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Yes, Fairview is beginner-friendly and family-suitable. The terrain is gentle with short walking distances (typically under 2 miles), and specimens are relatively easy to spot in washes. The area is rarely crowded, making it a relaxed introduction to rockhounding. Children ages 6 and up can participate safely.

faq.question: What tools and techniques work best for finding agate at Fairview?
faq.answer: Carry a 10x loupe to examine specimens in place before collecting - this helps you identify high-quality pieces with good color and banding. Search wash bottoms and banks where water has concentrated material. Bring a rock hammer and cold chisel for extracting embedded specimens from outcrops, though most surface finds can be hand-collected. Wear sturdy boots for the rocky terrain.

meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding at Fairview, Utah. Find agate, jasper, and quartz. No permit required, 25 lb daily limit. Family-friendly near Manti.
